content.code:
for (init-statement; condition; end-expression)
   statement;

content.text: Key insightThe order of execution for the different parts of a for-statement is as follows:Init-statementCondition (if this is false, the loop terminates here).Loop bodyEnd-expression (then jumps back to condition)Note that the end-expression executesafterthe loop statement, and then the condition is re-evaluated.

The order of execution for the different parts of a for-statement is as follows:Init-statementCondition (if this is false, the loop terminates here).Loop bodyEnd-expression (then jumps back to condition)Note that the end-expression executesafterthe loop statement, and then the condition is re-evaluated.

list: Init-statementCondition (if this is false, the loop terminates here).Loop bodyEnd-expression (then jumps back to condition)

Loop bodyEnd-expression (then jumps back to condition)

content.text: Note that the end-expression executesafterthe loop statement, and then the condition is re-evaluated.
Best practiceAvoidoperator!=when doing numeric comparisons in the for-loop condition. Preferoperator<oroperator<=where possible.
Avoidoperator!=when doing numeric comparisons in the for-loop condition. Preferoperator<oroperator<=where possible.

content.code:
for (;;)
    statement;

while (true)
    statement;

content.text: Related contentWe cover the comma operator in lesson6.5 -- The comma operator.
We cover the comma operator in lesson6.5 -- The comma operator.
Best practiceDefining multiple variables (in the init-statement) and using the comma operator (in the end-expression) is acceptable inside a for-statement.
Defining multiple variables (in the init-statement) and using the comma operator (in the end-expression) is acceptable inside a for-statement.

list: It makes our program more complex, as we have to read more code to see where the variable is used.It may actually be slower because the compiler may not be able to optimize a variable with a larger scope as effectively.
It may actually be slower because the compiler may not be able to optimize a variable with a larger scope as effectively.

content.text: Best practiceVariables used only inside a loop should be defined inside the scope of the loop.
Variables used only inside a loop should be defined inside the scope of the loop.
Best practicePrefer for-loops over while-loops when there is an obvious loop variable.Prefer while-loops over for-loops when there is no obvious loop variable.
Prefer for-loops over while-loops when there is an obvious loop variable.Prefer while-loops over for-loops when there is no obvious loop variable.

content.code:
for (int i{ 0 }; i <= 20; i += 2)
    std::cout << i << '\n';

content.text: This for-loop executes as long asi >= 0. In other words, it runs untiliis negative. However, becauseiis unsigned,ican never go negative. Consequently, this loop will run for-ever (ha ha)! Generally, it’s a good idea to avoid looping on unsigned variables unless necessary.
